text: In this video we report details of robotic transabdominal enucleation of a large symptomatic rectal leiomyoma in a fifty-four years old female patient.
The patient was admitted in our department complaining pain in the right inguinal crural region.
During the proctologic examination, a submucosal round mass is visually identified in the posterior right rectal region.
Preoperative endoscopy, computed tomography scan and transanal ultrasound confirmed a submucosal mass of seven centimeters in diameter, located in the pelvis.
A preoperative biopsy was non-diagnostic.
The patient was planned for a robotic enucleation of the mass in an attempt to define the histology and relieve the symptoms.
The patient is positioned under general anesthesia in a thirty-degree Trendelenburg position with legs apart, preparing for the surgical procedure.
The surgeon establishes a pneumoperitoneum at twelve millimeters of mercury using a blind technique, preparing the abdominal cavity for robotic access.
The surgeon places the camera under direct vision and performs an inspection of the abdominal cavity, observing the internal structures and organs.
Four robotic trocars and two assisting trocars are inserted into the patient's abdomen, preparing for the robotic enucleation procedure.
The surgeon uses the fourth robotic port to apply traction on the tissue using a fenestrated grasper, facilitating exposure and manipulation during the procedure.
The assistant applies countertraction using laparoscopic instruments, aiding in the manipulation of tissues within the surgical field.
The surgeon retracts the sigmoid colon and dissects the posterolateral peritoneum to enter a safe plane of dissection, using robotic and laparoscopic instruments.
The surgeon dissects the tumor located in the right posterolateral wall of the rectum under direct vision, utilizing monopolar scissors and fenestrated bipolar forceps.
The surgeon begins isolating the mass by detaching its adhesions from the mesorectal tissue using precise dissection techniques.
The surgeon performs enucleation of the mass using a combination of sharp and blunt dissection techniques, carefully separating the tumor from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon sections near-formed vascular pedicles using laparoscopic instruments, following the application of countertraction.
The mass is visually observed displacing the rectum anteriorly and to the left, altering its anatomical position within the surgical field.
The surgeon applies cranial traction using a transfixed stitch to facilitate the enucleation of the tumor located in the right posterolateral wall of the rectum.
The surgeon continues the dissection inferiorly, reaching the pelvic floor, with visible separation of tissue layers in the pelvic region.
The surgeon completes the excision of the mass by detaching it from the fibers of the muscular wall of the rectum, using precise dissection techniques.
The surgeon ensures safe margins from surrounding structures and maintains the integrity of the rectal mucosa, avoiding any iatrogenic penetration of the pseudocapsule.
The surgeon uses a laparoscopic entrapment bag to retrieve the excised specimen from the surgical field.
The surgeon sutures the rectal muscular defect using 3-0 absorbable stitches, applying a two-layer closure technique to ensure proper healing.
The surgeon performs a hydroneumatic test on the sutured rectal muscular defect, confirming the absence of rectal leakage.
Following a hemostatic review, the surgeon places a drain in the pelvic cavity to manage postoperative fluid accumulation.
